content.text: Before a Data Protection Impact Assessment can be conducted, the organisation must determine whether one is legally required. This screening step — the DPIA trigger assessment — applies a structured series of questions to any proposed agentic AI feature, correlating its characteristics against the high-risk processing criteria set out in GDPR Article 35 and supplementary guidance from supervisory authorities. For agentic systems the standard criteria are necessary but not sufficient: their capacity to autonomously call external tools, maintain persistent memory, chain outputs across agent boundaries, and take actions in the real world creates risk vectors that predate regulatory frameworks and require additional assessment factors specific to AI agency.
What It Is

A DPIA trigger assessment is a lightweight pre-screening process that determines whether a planned processing activity meets the threshold for requiring a full Data Protection Impact Assessment under Article 35 of the GDPR. The legal test has two gates: the processing must be likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, and it must fall within at least one of the enumerated high-risk categories — systematic and extensive profiling, processing of special category data at scale, or systematic monitoring of a publicly accessible area, among others. The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) has published nine criteria for assessing "high risk"; two or more criteria being present is a strong indicator that a DPIA is mandatory.

For agentic AI features the standard trigger assessment framework requires extension. A traditional screening checklist might conclude no DPIA is required because the processing volume is low or no special category data is explicitly stored. But an agentic system that autonomously invokes external APIs, caches intermediate reasoning over long sessions, or chains outputs into downstream decision-making processes can create emergent privacy risks not present in the individual components. The DPIA trigger assessment for agentic AI therefore layers an agentic risk multiplier scoring step on top of the standard GDPR high-risk criteria, so that features with elevated agentic risk characteristics are flagged for full DPIA even when the primary data volumes appear modest.

The output of a trigger assessment is one of three determinations: full DPIA required (engage the Data Protection Officer within a defined window), lightweight Privacy Impact Assessment sufficient (no DPO engagement mandatory but internal documentation required), or no formal assessment required (document the negative determination and retain for accountability purposes). Each determination must be documented in the organisation's Record of Processing Activities (RoPA).
Why It Matters

Conducting a DPIA when required by law is not optional — Article 83(4) of the GDPR provides for fines of up to €10 million or 2% of global annual turnover for failure to carry out a DPIA where one was legally required. More practically, the trigger assessment is the earliest point in the feature delivery cycle at which privacy risk can be identified and designed out, rather than retrofitted after implementation. Features that enter development without a trigger assessment check often encounter requirements for architectural changes late in the delivery cycle, when the cost of redesign is highest.

For agentic AI specifically, the stakes are elevated because the risk surface is difficult to enumerate from a high-level feature description. An agent that appears to process only non-personal data in isolation may process personal data in combination when it retrieves user context from a memory store, queries an external CRM, or synthesises outputs from multiple tool calls. The trigger assessment creates a forcing function for the engineering and product teams to enumerate, at the point of feature specification, every data source the agent will access, every tool it may invoke, and every downstream system that will receive its outputs — information that should inform the technical architecture regardless of privacy obligations.
Benefits
Legal compliance:

The trigger assessment provides documented evidence that the organisation evaluated the need for a DPIA, satisfying the accountability principle under GDPR Article 5(2) and creating a defensible audit trail for supervisory authority inspections.
Early risk identification:

Privacy risks identified during feature specification can be eliminated through architectural choices (e.g. not retaining session context beyond the immediate turn) at near-zero cost; the same risk discovered post-launch requires data deletion, system redesign, and user notification.
Scope clarity:

The data enumeration required during trigger assessment forces product teams to articulate precisely what personal data the agent will touch, which is often underspecified in initial product requirements and leads to data minimisation improvements regardless of DPIA outcome.
DPO resourcing:

A structured trigger assessment gates DPO involvement to features that genuinely require it, preventing both under-consultation (missing mandatory DPO reviews) and over-consultation (wasting DPO capacity on routine features).
Supplier due diligence:

The trigger assessment process identifies which third-party AI providers, vector databases, and tool APIs will be processing personal data on behalf of the organisation, triggering Data Processing Agreement (DPA) requirements before development begins.
Trade-offs
False positives:

A conservative trigger assessment calibrated for agentic risk will flag some features for full DPIA that a narrower reading of Article 35 might not require. This creates process overhead. The alternative — a permissive threshold — risks missing mandatory DPIAs.
Assessment expertise:

Trigger assessments for agentic AI require assessors who understand both GDPR criteria and agentic system architecture. Standard privacy engineers may under-weight agentic multipliers; pure AI engineers may not know the regulatory criteria. Organisations often lack staff with both competencies.
Process friction:

Mandating a trigger assessment for every agentic feature can slow delivery cycles if the process is manual and heavyweight. Lightweight automation (a structured questionnaire with routing logic) reduces friction but requires ongoing maintenance as the regulatory landscape evolves.
Scope creep during development:

A trigger assessment conducted at specification time may become stale if the feature scope expands during development. Organisations must establish change control triggers that require reassessment when the feature's data access or tool permissions materially change.
How It Works

The trigger assessment operates in three sequential stages. The first stage applies the base personal data test: does the feature process personal data (directly or indirectly) at all? If the answer is no, the assessment terminates with a documented no-DPIA determination. If yes, the assessment proceeds to stage two.

The second stage applies the GDPR Article 35 high-risk criteria. The EDPB guidelines (WP248 rev.01) identify nine criteria that indicate high risk. Two or more triggered criteria create a presumption that a DPIA is required. The criteria most relevant to agentic AI are: (1) evaluation or scoring — an agent that generates inferences about individuals based on their behaviour or interaction history; (2) automated decision-making with legal or similarly significant effect — an agent whose outputs directly influence access to services, pricing, or employment decisions; (3) systematic monitoring — an agent that continuously observes user behaviour across sessions; (4) sensitive data — an agent that processes health, financial, or location data as inputs or generates them as outputs; (5) large scale — an agent deployed to a large user base or processing high volumes of interactions.

The third stage applies agentic risk multipliers — factors that are not present in the standard GDPR criteria but are material to agentic systems. Each multiplier is scored on a 0–3 scale: 0 (not present), 1 (low exposure), 2 (moderate exposure), 3 (high exposure). A total multiplier score above a threshold, or any single factor scoring 3, escalates the determination to full DPIA regardless of the stage two outcome. The multiplier factors are: autonomous tool invocation breadth, persistent memory scope, multi-agent chaining depth, data exfiltration pathway count, implicit profiling from interaction patterns, and real-world actuation capability.

The risk multiplier approach follows the logic that agentic systems can amplify conventional processing risks through combination and autonomy. An agent that accesses a CRM, a calendar, and an email system individually triggers no unusual privacy concerns; the same agent autonomously combining those sources to infer personal relationships, schedules, and communication patterns creates a processing activity that exceeds what any single data source would suggest. The multiplier framework forces assessors to evaluate the emergent risk of combination, not just each data access in isolation.
Architecture

The screening workflow routes each new agentic AI feature through three decision gates. A "No" exit at the first gate (systematic or large-scale personal data processing) terminates the assessment with a documented no-DPIA decision. A "No" at the second gate (GDPR Art 35 high-risk criterion) routes to the agentic risk multiplier panel. A score below threshold from the multipliers results in a lightweight PIA; a score at or above threshold merges back into the full DPIA path.

attrs.alt: DPIA trigger screening workflow with three decision gates: personal data processing, GDPR Art 35 high-risk criteria, and agentic risk multiplier scoring

content.text: When to Use It

A DPIA trigger assessment should be initiated at the feature specification stage, before any design or development work begins. The correct integration point is the product requirements review — the assessment is a gate that must be passed before a feature is added to a development sprint. Organisations using RFC or design document processes should embed the trigger assessment as a mandatory section in the design document template.

The trigger assessment must also be repeated when an existing feature undergoes material scope changes. Material changes for agentic AI include: adding new tool integrations that provide access to personal data, expanding the user population (particularly into jurisdictions with stricter data protection regimes), extending session memory retention beyond what was originally assessed, adding multi-agent orchestration where the agent's outputs feed into further automated processing, and changing the legal basis for processing.

The trigger assessment is not appropriate as a substitute for full security risk assessment, threat modelling, or red-team exercises. Its scope is specifically the privacy risk to data subjects. Organisations should run the DPIA trigger assessment in parallel with — not instead of — their standard security review processes.
How to Implement

Implementation begins with creating a structured questionnaire that maps to the three assessment stages. The questionnaire should be hosted in the organisation's ticketing or compliance platform so that each trigger assessment produces a traceable record linked to the feature's development ticket. Responses to the questionnaire drive automated routing logic that produces the preliminary determination; a privacy engineer then reviews and approves or overrides the automated result.

The agentic risk multiplier scoring table should be calibrated to the organisation's deployment context. A consumer-facing agent handling health data warrants lower thresholds (score 1 triggers escalation) than an internal productivity agent handling scheduling data (score 2 required). The calibration decision should be made by the DPO and documented as organisational policy so assessors apply it consistently.
Step-by-Step Trigger Assessment

Enumerate all personal data the agent will access: direct inputs (user messages, uploaded files), retrieved context (memory store, user profile, CRM records), tool outputs (API responses containing personal data), and inferred data (profiling outputs generated by the agent's reasoning).

Apply the systematic/large-scale test: is the processing systematic (structured, methodical, part of a general plan) rather than occasional? Does it affect a large number of data subjects or a large volume of data? If both answers are no, document the no-DPIA determination with rationale.

Score each EDPB Art 35 criterion: evaluate-or-score (0/1), automated-significant-decision (0/1), systematic-monitoring (0/1), sensitive-data (0/1), large-scale (0/1), data-matching-or-combining (0/1), vulnerable-subjects (0/1), innovative-tech (0/1), cross-border-transfer (0/1). Record the score and supporting rationale for each.

Score each agentic multiplier: tool-invocation-breadth (0–3), memory-persistence-scope (0–3), multi-agent-chain-depth (0–3), exfiltration-pathway-count (0–3), implicit-profiling-capability (0–3), real-world-actuation (0–3). Sum the scores.

Apply routing logic: if Art 35 score ≥ 2 OR agentic total ≥ 6 OR any single agentic factor = 3, determination is full DPIA required; if Art 35 score = 1 OR agentic total 3–5, determination is lightweight PIA; otherwise document no-DPIA with rationale.
Record the determination in the RoPA, link the record to the development ticket, and notify the DPO if a full DPIA is required.
Examples
Introductory: Agentic Risk Multiplier Scorer

The following example implements the agentic risk multiplier scoring step as a Python dataclass. Each factor is scored independently, and the class provides the routing determination based on configurable thresholds.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional


class DPIADetermination(Enum):
    FULL_DPIA_REQUIRED = "full_dpia_required"
    LIGHTWEIGHT_PIA = "lightweight_pia"
    NO_ASSESSMENT_REQUIRED = "no_assessment_required"


@dataclass
class AgenticRiskMultipliers:
    """Score each factor 0-3: 0=not present, 1=low, 2=moderate, 3=high."""
    tool_invocation_breadth: int = 0      # number and sensitivity of tools accessible
    memory_persistence_scope: int = 0    # how long and how broadly context is retained
    multi_agent_chain_depth: int = 0     # how many agent hops outputs traverse
    exfiltration_pathway_count: int = 0  # number of external endpoints the agent can write to
    implicit_profiling_capability: int = 0  # ability to infer attributes from behaviour
    real_world_actuation: int = 0        # can the agent take actions in external systems

    escalation_threshold: int = field(default=6, repr=False)
    pia_threshold: int = field(default=3, repr=False)

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        for factor_name in [
            "tool_invocation_breadth", "memory_persistence_scope",
            "multi_agent_chain_depth", "exfiltration_pathway_count",
            "implicit_profiling_capability", "real_world_actuation",
        ]:
            v = getattr(self, factor_name)
            if not 0 <= v <= 3:
                raise ValueError(f"{factor_name} must be 0-3, got {v}")

    @property
    def total_score(self) -> int:
        return (
            self.tool_invocation_breadth
            + self.memory_persistence_scope
            + self.multi_agent_chain_depth
            + self.exfiltration_pathway_count
            + self.implicit_profiling_capability
            + self.real_world_actuation
        )

    @property
    def any_factor_critical(self) -> bool:
        return any(
            getattr(self, f) == 3
            for f in [
                "tool_invocation_breadth", "memory_persistence_scope",
                "multi_agent_chain_depth", "exfiltration_pathway_count",
                "implicit_profiling_capability", "real_world_actuation",
            ]
        )

    def determination(self, art35_score: int) -> DPIADetermination:
        if art35_score >= 2 or self.total_score >= self.escalation_threshold or self.any_factor_critical:
            return DPIADetermination.FULL_DPIA_REQUIRED
        if art35_score >= 1 or self.total_score >= self.pia_threshold:
            return DPIADetermination.LIGHTWEIGHT_PIA
        return DPIADetermination.NO_ASSESSMENT_REQUIRED


# Example: customer-facing scheduling agent with calendar and email tool access
multipliers = AgenticRiskMultipliers(
    tool_invocation_breadth=2,     # reads calendar + email
    memory_persistence_scope=1,    # retains context within session only
    multi_agent_chain_depth=0,     # single agent, no chaining
    exfiltration_pathway_count=1,  # can compose and queue emails
    implicit_profiling_capability=2,  # infers schedule patterns, relationships
    real_world_actuation=2,        # can book meetings, send emails
)
print(f"Total agentic score: {multipliers.total_score}")  # 8
print(f"Any critical factor: {multipliers.any_factor_critical}")  # False
print(multipliers.determination(art35_score=1))  # FULL_DPIA_REQUIRED (score >= 6)
Realistic: Structured Trigger Assessment Record

In a production setting the trigger assessment is implemented as a structured form with automated routing and an audit trail. The following example shows a complete trigger assessment record model with serialisation to a RoPA-compatible JSON format.

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Optional
from uuid import uuid4


@dataclass
class Art35CriteriaScores:
    """EDPB WP248 rev.01 nine criteria. Each is 0 (not triggered) or 1 (triggered)."""
    evaluate_or_score: int = 0
    automated_significant_decision: int = 0
    systematic_monitoring: int = 0
    sensitive_data: int = 0
    large_scale: int = 0
    data_matching_or_combining: int = 0
    vulnerable_subjects: int = 0
    innovative_technology: int = 0
    cross_border_transfer: int = 0

    @property
    def total(self) -> int:
        return sum(asdict(self).values())


@dataclass
class DPIATriggerRecord:
    feature_id: str
    feature_name: str
    assessor_email: str
    processes_personal_data: bool
    art35_criteria: Art35CriteriaScores
    agentic_multipliers: AgenticRiskMultipliers
    determination: DPIADetermination
    rationale: str
    dpo_notified: bool = False
    dpo_notification_date: Optional[str] = None
    assessed_at: str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat())
    record_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid4()))

    def notify_dpo(self) -> None:
        if self.determination != DPIADetermination.FULL_DPIA_REQUIRED:
            raise ValueError("DPO notification is only required for full DPIA determinations")
        # In production: send email via org notification system
        self.dpo_notified = True
        self.dpo_notification_date = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()

    def to_ropa_json(self) -> str:
        return json.dumps({
            "record_id": self.record_id,
            "feature_id": self.feature_id,
            "feature_name": self.feature_name,
            "assessed_at": self.assessed_at,
            "assessor": self.assessor_email,
            "processes_personal_data": self.processes_personal_data,
            "art35_score": self.art35_criteria.total,
            "art35_criteria": asdict(self.art35_criteria),
            "agentic_total_score": self.agentic_multipliers.total_score,
            "agentic_any_critical": self.agentic_multipliers.any_factor_critical,
            "agentic_multipliers": {
                k: v for k, v in asdict(self.agentic_multipliers).items()
                if not k.endswith("_threshold")
            },
            "determination": self.determination.value,
            "rationale": self.rationale,
            "dpo_notified": self.dpo_notified,
            "dpo_notification_date": self.dpo_notification_date,
        }, indent=2)


# Full assessment for an AI research assistant with web search and knowledge base tools
assessment = DPIATriggerRecord(
    feature_id="feat-2024-ai-research-assistant",
    feature_name="AI Research Assistant with Web Search",
    assessor_email="privacy-eng@example.com",
    processes_personal_data=True,
    art35_criteria=Art35CriteriaScores(
        evaluate_or_score=1,       # generates summaries that may profile user interests
        innovative_technology=1,   # novel LLM-based approach
        data_matching_or_combining=1,  # combines web results with user history
    ),
    agentic_multipliers=AgenticRiskMultipliers(
        tool_invocation_breadth=2,
        memory_persistence_scope=2,    # cross-session search history retained
        exfiltration_pathway_count=1,
        implicit_profiling_capability=2,
    ),
    determination=DPIADetermination.FULL_DPIA_REQUIRED,
    rationale="Art 35 score 3 (>=2) plus agentic total 7 (>=6). Cross-session memory with profiling capability requires full DPIA.",
)
assessment.notify_dpo()
print(assessment.to_ropa_json())
Real-World Example

A European fintech deploying an AI-powered personal finance agent runs the trigger assessment for a new feature: the agent will analyse transaction history to proactively identify unusual spending patterns and alert the user. The Art 35 criteria analysis triggers three criteria: evaluate-or-score (it profiles spending behaviour), sensitive-data (financial data qualifies as Category A data under some national implementations), and large-scale (the platform has 400,000 active users). The Art 35 score of 3 immediately requires a full DPIA.

The agentic multiplier assessment adds further weight: tool-invocation-breadth scores 2 (the agent accesses transaction data and a notification system), memory-persistence-scope scores 3 (the agent maintains a 90-day rolling transaction window in a vector store), and implicit-profiling-capability scores 3 (inferring income, household composition, and health conditions from spending patterns is explicitly possible at this data granularity). The any_factor_critical flag is true for two factors. Even had the Art 35 score been below threshold, the agentic multipliers would have mandated a full DPIA.

The DPIA resulting from this trigger assessment ultimately required three architectural changes: reducing the memory window from 90 days to 30 days, encrypting the vector store with per-user keys so the embedding layer cannot cross-reference users, and restricting the categories of inferences the agent is permitted to surface in its alerts. None of these changes would have been identified before deployment without the structured trigger assessment forcing the data access enumeration step.
Common Mistakes
Assessing Only Direct Data Inputs

Product teams frequently scope the trigger assessment to data the user explicitly provides to the agent, missing all indirect data sources. An agent that receives only a user's natural-language query appears low-risk in isolation. But if it retrieves user profile data from a database, pulls recent purchase history from an API, and logs the interaction for model retraining, the actual personal data processing scope is far larger than the user input alone. The data enumeration step in the trigger assessment must trace every system the agent reads from or writes to, not just its primary input channel.
Treating the Trigger Assessment as One-Off

Features evolve after the initial trigger assessment. An agent launched with three tools may acquire five additional tool integrations over six months of feature development; the original no-DPIA determination is no longer valid. Organisations must implement a change-trigger mechanism — ideally as a required field in the change request template — that re-runs the trigger assessment when a defined threshold of change has occurred.
Conflating DPIA Trigger with Full DPIA

The trigger assessment determines whether a DPIA is required — it is not the DPIA itself. A common failure mode is treating the trigger assessment questionnaire as a DPIA substitute, with teams marking the process complete after the trigger assessment without commissioning the full assessment. The trigger assessment output must explicitly state whether a full DPIA is required, and the project should not proceed to development until the DPO has either confirmed the trigger assessment determination or initiated the DPIA process.
Best Practices
Automate routing, require human sign-off:

Use a structured form with automated routing logic to generate a preliminary determination, but require a qualified privacy engineer to review and approve it. Automation ensures consistency; human review catches edge cases the questionnaire logic misses.
Enumerate data flows before scoring:
Require assessors to complete a full data flow diagram or data inventory before filling in the scoring criteria. Without the data enumeration step, assessors cannot score criteria accurately.
Calibrate agentic thresholds to context:

Set agentic multiplier thresholds based on the sensitivity of the user population and the deployment context. Consumer health applications warrant lower thresholds than internal tooling; B2B enterprise deployments processing employees' data warrant lower thresholds than those handling end-customer data indirectly.
Integrate with supplier onboarding:

When the trigger assessment identifies a new AI provider or tool API that will process personal data, immediately initiate the DPA review and SCCs (Standard Contractual Clauses) assessment for that supplier in parallel with the DPIA process, not sequentially.
Version and retain all determinations:

Store every trigger assessment record with its version number, linking it to the feature release version it was conducted for. Supervisory authorities may request evidence that the organisation conducted a trigger assessment for a specific feature version, which requires version-linked records.
Performance and Scalability

At low feature velocity (fewer than ten new agentic features per month), manual trigger assessments with a structured template are operationally feasible. Each assessment typically requires two to four hours of a privacy engineer's time for data enumeration plus scoring, and thirty minutes of DPO review time if escalated.

At higher velocity — common in organisations with multiple product teams deploying AI features in parallel — the bottleneck is the DPO review step when multiple features simultaneously require full DPIA. Organisations that anticipate this should implement a tiered DPO engagement model: the DPO is notified of all full DPIA determinations, but routine high-risk features are reviewed by a designated privacy champion in the engineering team who operates under DPO delegation, reserving direct DPO engagement for novel or high-volume features.

The data enumeration step scales poorly when agent tool access is determined dynamically at runtime rather than statically at specification time. Organisations building agent platforms where tools are user-configurable must implement assessment policies that cover the maximum possible tool access scope, rather than attempting to assess each user configuration individually.
Tooling and Ecosystem

Privacy management platforms including OneTrust, TrustArc, and Privaci provide DPIA workflow modules with configurable questionnaire routing. These platforms automate the RoPA linkage and DPO notification but require customisation to incorporate agentic risk multiplier factors, as their default templates pre-date widespread agentic AI deployment.

The EDPB's DPIA self-assessment tool (available at edpb.europa.eu) implements the Article 35 criteria natively and can be used as a reference for calibrating in-house scoring logic. For organisations using Jira or ServiceNow, the trigger assessment can be implemented as a custom issue type with required fields mapped to each assessment criterion; automated routing rules drive the DPO notification workflow.
Comparison with Alternatives

The structured trigger assessment is one of several approaches organisations use to gate DPIA initiation. The alternatives represent different trade-offs between comprehensiveness, consistency, and operational overhead.
Approach
How It Works
Advantages
Disadvantages
Structured trigger assessment (this approach)
Scores Art 35 criteria plus agentic multipliers to produce a determination
Consistent, auditable, accounts for agentic risk vectors
Requires calibration; may produce false positives at conservative thresholds
Expert judgement
Privacy engineer or DPO makes a case-by-case determination
Handles novel features well; expert can weight contextual factors
Inconsistent across assessors; requires scarce expert time for every feature
Category-based mandatory DPIA
Any feature in a defined category (e.g. all AI features) mandatorily triggers a DPIA
Simple, no screening step needed, zero false-negative risk
High overhead; DPO resource consumed by low-risk features; impractical at scale
Risk appetite threshold
Only features above a revenue or user-count threshold are assessed
Easy to operationalise; no privacy expertise required for screening
Misaligned with GDPR criteria; small-scale features can be high-risk (e.g. processing health data at low volume)
Security and Reliability

The trigger assessment process itself handles sensitive information: the data flow enumeration step produces a document that catalogues all personal data sources the agent accesses, the number of users affected, and the sensitivity classification of that data. This enumeration document must be stored in access-controlled systems with audit logging, as it constitutes a detailed map of the organisation's data processing activities that would be valuable to an attacker scoping a data theft or insider threat.

The reliability of the trigger assessment as a compliance mechanism depends on its integration with the development workflow being mandatory and enforceable. Optional or advisory trigger assessments are routinely skipped under delivery pressure. The assessment must be a hard gate in the sprint acceptance process — a feature without a completed trigger assessment record must not be accepted into development. This requires tooling integration (the ticket system blocks sprint assignment without the trigger assessment record ID) rather than policy alone.

Consider the scenario where an adversary manipulates the trigger assessment routing logic — for example, by crafting a feature description that under-reports tool access scope. Defence in depth requires that the DPO review step includes a spot-check of the data enumeration against the actual technical specification, not just the assessor's self-reported responses. Regular audits of trigger assessment records against deployed system configurations catch discrepancies before they become compliance findings.
Mental Model

Think of the DPIA trigger assessment as a building permit inspection before construction begins. A planning authority does not inspect every building project with the same depth; it uses a screening process to identify projects that require detailed safety review — large structures, unusual materials, or locations in flood zones — and fast-tracks low-risk projects through a lighter process. The screening criteria map to known risk factors; the detailed review (the DPIA, like the full structural inspection) is reserved for projects where the risk factors indicate it is necessary.

Agentic AI features are the equivalent of construction projects in flood zones: the base building regulations apply, but the inherent risk characteristics of the location (autonomy, tool access, persistent memory) mean the screening criteria must include location-specific factors that the generic rules do not capture. The agentic risk multipliers are those location-specific factors — they supplement the standard criteria rather than replacing them.
Key Takeaways

A DPIA trigger assessment is a mandatory screening step that determines whether a proposed processing activity meets the GDPR Article 35 threshold for a full Data Protection Impact Assessment; failure to conduct it when required can result in fines of up to €10 million or 2% of global turnover.
The EDPB identifies nine Article 35 criteria for high-risk processing; triggering two or more creates a strong presumption that a full DPIA is required.

Agentic AI features require supplementary risk multiplier scoring across six factors — tool invocation breadth, memory persistence scope, multi-agent chain depth, exfiltration pathway count, implicit profiling capability, and real-world actuation — because standard GDPR criteria do not capture emergent risks from autonomous operation and data combination.

The data flow enumeration step — tracing every data source the agent reads from or writes to, including indirect sources retrieved by tool calls — is the foundation of an accurate trigger assessment and is frequently underestimated by product teams.

The trigger assessment must be integrated as a hard gate in the development workflow, not an advisory process; tooling enforcement (blocking sprint acceptance without a completed record) is more reliable than policy alone.

The trigger assessment must be repeated when a feature's scope materially changes — new tool integrations, expanded user populations, extended memory retention, or added multi-agent chaining all require reassessment of the original determination.

The trigger assessment is not a substitute for the full DPIA — it only determines whether one is required; organisations must commission the full DPIA immediately on a full-DPIA determination and must not allow feature development to proceed until the DPO has acknowledged the requirement.
